summary:
The primary arteries supplying blood to the submandibular gland are branches of the external carotid artery system, specifically including the facial artery. This information aligns consistently across medical literature focusing on regional vasculature patterns for surgical planning or diagnostic purposes related to salivary glands' function and pathology evaluation.
The main source of arterial supply to the submandibular gland comes via branches of the external carotid artery, primarily through its largest terminal division—the facial artery—supporting standard anatomic teachings applicable clinically when assessing these glands’ perfusion needs during procedures like surgery or diagnosis involving them. To summarize Agent1’s reasoning into two concise sentences focused only on critical clinical aspects regarding the arterial supply of the submandibular gland:

- Primary arterial supply originates from branches of the external carotid artery, most importantly the facial artery.
- Clinical significance includes understanding perfusion pathways crucial for surgeries or diagnoses concerning the submandibular gland.
